architecture.summary: MoRFI is a two-stage mechanistic interpretability pipeline that identifies SAE latent features causally linked to fine-tuning-induced hallucinations in LLMs. Stage 1 (Algorithm 1) performs bootstrapped monotonic trend detection across a 4D activation tensor (samples × property configurations × latent features × fine-tuning epochs), using Spearman rank and Mann-Kendall tests to isolate features whose activations change monotonically with controlled properties (proportion of unknown facts, training epochs). Stage 2 (Algorithm 2) validates candidate latents through activation steering on a reference task, applying interventions to the residual stream and ranking by downstream performance impact. The pipeline bridges external SAE representations into model activation space via decoder projections, enabling causal attribution of knowledge degradation to sparse latent directions.

biblio.title: MoRFI: Monotonic Sparse Autoencoder Feature Identification

claims.statement: Incrementally introducing new knowledge increases hallucinations across Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B and Mistral 7B v03, with the effect being more pronounced with prolonged training.
69–85% of facts recovered through single-latent steering interventions align with those accessible to a baseline fine-tuned on known facts.
Single-latent interventions sourced with MoRFI substantially outperform steering with the composite direction (difference-in-means SAE activations between known vs. unknown checkpoints).

For the Unknown direction, the best negatively steered latent gives +33.4 accuracy gain while the best positively steered one gives +21.7 for Llama 3.1 8B; for Mistral 7B the difference is +39.77 vs +24.91 and for Gemma 2 9B +18.46 vs +9.03.

Exposure to unknown facts disrupts the model's ability to retrieve stored knowledge along a set of directions in the residual stream; steering experiments suggest forgetting reflects disrupted access rather than erasure of parametric knowledge.

Within-group cosine similarity of top-performing latents is generally low (0.3), indicating that the top-performing latents span a distributed subspace at the middle layer rather than clustering along a shared low-rank structure.

Steering with control latents that exhibit no significant monotonic trend produces negligible or highly negative accuracy changes, whereas trending latents produce substantial gains, confirming that performance improvements are not artifacts of arbitrary residual stream perturbation.

MoRFI reliably discovers causally relevant latents across distinct models (Llama 3.1 8B, Gemma 2 9B, Mistral 7B v03), and there is significant overlap between latents surfaced across the Unknown and Epochs dimensions, suggesting they capture a shared signal.
